elements.sent-2411-021: One particularly challenging domain for LVLMs involves the analysis of geometric structures, especially graphs.
elements.sent-2411-022: The inherent complexity of graphs stems from their flexible nature, where nodes and edges can be arranged in countless configurations, creating potentially intricate patterns.

elements.sent-2411-026: Our research seeks to address the following two fundamental questions:.
elements.sent-2411-027: Primary question: How robust are LVLMs in analyzing visual graphs?.
elements.sent-2411-028: Secondary question: To what extent does the choice of visual style---such as node color, node labels, or layout---impact LVLM performance?.
elements.sent-2411-029: To investigate these, we developed \visgraphvar\ (Visual Graph Variability), a visual graph benchmark generator capable of producing graph images with varying styles and structures.
elements.sent-2411-030: We developed \visgraphvar\ under the assumption that a benchmark is only good if, for its generation, lessons learned from previous benchmarks are taken into account.
elements.sent-2411-031: While existing benchmarks for graph image analysis often focus on single tasks like reasoning~\cite{wei2024gitagraphvisualtextual and overlook style variations~\cite{Williams2024-rp,li2024visiongraphleveraginglargemultimodal , \visgraphvar\ operates across multiple dimensions.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: Inspired by the work of~\cite{Chartrand2012-iz , we divide our analysis into seven dimensions, each examining a specific task related to visual graph interpretation:.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Question: How many nodes and edges does the graph contain?.
elements.sent-2411-036: Question: What type of graph is shown in the image?.
elements.sent-2411-037: Question: Which edges of the graph are cut-edges?.
elements.sent-2411-038: Question: Which patterns can be identified in the graph, and how many instances of each?.
elements.sent-2411-039: Question: Based on the graph's structure, which nodes are likely to form new connections?.
elements.sent-2411-040: Question: Which one is the shortest path between two specified nodes?.
elements.sent-2411-041: Question: Are the two displayed graphs equal to each other (apart from colors and styles)?.
elements.sent-2411-042: Through this systematic approach, \visgraphvar\ provides a comprehensive evaluation methodology capable of better capturing the strengths and weaknesses of current LVLMs in visual graph analysis tasks.
elements.sent-2411-043: This multi-dimensional strategy offers a more precise insight on how LVLMs perform across different aspects of graph interpretation and understanding.

elements.sent-2411-078: Previous graph image benchmarks have largely overlooked the importance of graph layout, typically resorting to aesthetically pleasing arrangements or using standard library defaults (e.g., NetworkX in Python).
elements.sent-2411-079: However, since their training data influences LLVMs' inferences, the spatial arrangement of nodes and edges can significantly impact their ability to detect evident visual patterns.
elements.sent-2411-080: In general, layout selection is crucial in many graph applications because the inherent flexibility of graph structures allows us to emphasize specific characteristics over others.
elements.sent-2411-082: The spring layout effectively highlights nodes with high connectivity centrality.
elements.sent-2411-083: A circular layout excels at visualizing disconnected components by maintaining their visibility while keeping them organized separately from the main graph.
elements.sent-2411-084: A spectral layout effectively reveals community structures and node clusters by positioning nodes based on global connectivity patterns \end{itemize The choice of layout, therefore, depends on which graph properties need emphasis~\cite{10.5555/2381255.2381265 .}}
elements.sent-2411-085: Consequently, if an LLVM fails to perform consistently across different layouts, its practical applications could be significantly limited.
elements.sent-2411-086: This layout sensitivity becomes particularly critical when the same graph structure, presented in different spatial arrangements, yields varying inference results.

elements.sent-2411-098: A proficient LVLM for graph analysis should be able to accurately detect and count existing elements before progressing to more complex reasoning tasks.

elements.sent-2411-105: Visual benchmarks often assume that visual representations are flawless~\cite{wei2024gitagraphvisualtextual, li2024visiongraphleveraginglargemultimodal .}
elements.sent-2411-106: In reality, visual imperfections are common, especially as networks grow larger, and can occur either intentionally or unintentionally.
elements.sent-2411-108: Since humans naturally adapt to and work around these visual defects, \visgraphvar\ deliberately incorporates and sometimes introduces such imperfections into the generated graph images.
elements.sent-2411-109: Node and/or edge overlap is a prime example of these real-world visual challenges.
elements.sent-2411-110: In our evaluation approach, we expect LVLMs to provide counts that closely approximate the actual number of elements, even when some are overlapping.
elements.sent-2411-111: For instance, if an image contains 10 nodes with three overlapping ones, an LVLM that detects 9 nodes demonstrates better performance than one that only identifies 8.

elements.sent-2411-113: We expect LVLMs to match or exceed this human capability, particularly in cases of slight overlap (see Figure~\ref{fig:task1-overlap ).}

elements.sent-2411-115: In addition to mastering the task of detecting and counting nodes and edges, LVLMs should be able to classify a graph in the sense of determining its type.

elements.sent-2411-122: To evaluate an LVLM's proficiency in understanding these higher-order relationships between nodes and edges, we examine its ability to classify seven fundamental graph types (see Figure~\ref{fig:task2 ):.}
elements.sent-2411-123: Acyclic graphs: graphs without loops, that is, without any path starting from a node and returning to that node.
elements.sent-2411-124: Cyclic graphs: graphs containing one or more loops.
elements.sent-2411-125: Bipartite graphs: the set of nodes is partitioned into two distinct node sets with edges only between these two sets.
elements.sent-2411-126: Complete graphs: every node connects to all others.
elements.sent-2411-128: Planar graphs: graphs that can be drawn without edge crossings.
elements.sent-2411-129: Tree: Hierarchical graphs branching from a root.
elements.sent-2411-130: Each graph type presents unique analytical challenges.
elements.sent-2411-131: The selection included graphs that are clearly distinct from each other, such as trees and complete graphs, as well as graphs that are similar but with subtle differences, such as cyclic and acyclic graphs.
elements.sent-2411-132: This variety augments the thoroughness of the LVLM's analysis.
elements.sent-2411-133: For example, identifying cycles requires the model to trace edge paths and detect closed loops, while recognizing bipartite structures demands understanding node groupings and their interconnection patterns.
elements.sent-2411-134: Similarly, tree classification requires comprehending hierarchical relationships and directional flow from root to leaves.
elements.sent-2411-135: A LVLM should possess these analytical capabilities to accurately classify graph structures and understand their inherent properties.
elements.sent-2411-136: This understanding forms a crucial foundation for more complex visual reasoning tasks.
elements.sent-2411-137: The VisionGraph benchmark~\cite{li2024visiongraphleveraginglargemultimodal , for example, includes a classification task within its reasoning tasks, specifically to detect the presence of a cycle in a graph.}
elements.sent-2411-138: In contrast, our benchmark generator offers six additional types of graphs for classification.
elements.sent-2411-139: This difference arises because VisionGraph emphasizes reasoning tasks, whereas \visgraphvar\ reserves reasoning tasks primarily for Task 7; see Section~\ref{reasoning .}
elements.sent-2411-140: Beyond the classification of graphs, LVLMs must identify critical regions within graph images, such as cut-edges (or bridges).
elements.sent-2411-141: A cut-edge is an edge whose removal increases the graph's number of connected components---effectively segmenting the graph into two or more disconnected subgraphs~\cite{Grinberg2023-wj, Benjamin2015-kj .}

elements.sent-2411-161: Apart from memory retention and pattern recognition, there are other crucial tasks in graph analysis.
elements.sent-2411-162: A vital aspect concerns reasoning, with the example of link/edge prediction.
elements.sent-2411-163: When examining a graph, its structure and the positioning of nodes and edges (determined by the layout) may potentially suggest missing connections.
elements.sent-2411-164: By analyzing the overall topology, we can identify nodes with similar structural patterns that could logically be connected.
elements.sent-2411-165: This concept, known as link prediction, emerges when nodes sharing similar topological characteristics are not yet connected but show potential for connection~\cite{mao2024revisitinglinkpredictiondata .}
elements.sent-2411-166: Interestingly, the complexity of link prediction does not necessarily increase with the number of nodes (see Figure~\ref{fig:task5 ).}
elements.sent-2411-167: Smaller graphs can sometimes present greater challenges for prediction.
elements.sent-2411-168: This counterintuitive situation occurs when the model struggles to identify replicable patterns due to fewer examples of existing connections.
elements.sent-2411-169: The sparsity of edges in smaller graphs can make it harder to establish reliable prediction patterns.
elements.sent-2411-170: Link prediction differs from other graph analysis tasks as it requires a deeper level of reasoning from LVLMs.
elements.sent-2411-171: Rather than simply recognizing existing patterns or memorizing structures, the model must perform the following steps:.
elements.sent-2411-173: Identify similar topological patterns across different node pairs.
elements.sent-2411-174: Make logical inferences about potential connections.
elements.sent-2411-175: Consider both local and global network characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-176: Apply structural similarity principles to predict missing links.

elements.sent-2411-177: Currently, numerous research efforts focus on graph link prediction, though these approaches typically work with graphs as mathematical objects rather than with visual representations~\cite{zhang2018linkpredictionbasedgraph,li2023evaluatinggraphneuralnetworks .}
elements.sent-2411-178: The advanced reasoning requirement makes link prediction a particularly valuable benchmark for evaluating an LVLM's capability to understand and reason about complex network structures.
elements.sent-2411-179: If link prediction already requires the model to perform a certain level of reasoning, asking it to apply an algorithm to the graph displayed in the image significantly increases the difficulty.
elements.sent-2411-180: This is no longer just a visual task that can be inferred from the position of nodes and edges.
elements.sent-2411-181: An example is the problem of finding shortest paths, where the model must identify nodes, detect edge directions, store edge weights in memory, and then determine the shortest path between a source and destination.
elements.sent-2411-182: This task will test both the reasoning and memorization capabilities of the LVLMs.
elements.sent-2411-183: Moreover, this type of task requires applying numerical analysis to visual information.
elements.sent-2411-184: Generally, LLMs struggle with reasoning because their inferences are purely statistical and based on their training data~\cite{huang2023reasoninglargelanguagemodels .}
elements.sent-2411-185: This challenge is very complex for LVLMs because, in addition to processing text input, the model must reason about an image containing many visual elements, each of which can play a different role and change the ``direction'' of the reasoning process.
elements.sent-2411-186: In contrast to LLMs that generally only receive textual data to make inferences, an LVLM has to simultaneously process and reason about visual elements in an image, understand how they relate to each other, and integrate this understanding with any textual input---making the reasoning task substantially more complex.

elements.sent-2411-242: We employ three different metrics, which differ by task, so not all tasks share the same evaluation criteria.
elements.sent-2411-243: Each metric is normalized on a scale from 0 to 1, where 1 indicates optimal performance (complete alignment with the ground truth), and 0 indicates the lowest performance (no alignment with the ground truth).
elements.sent-2411-245: In Task 1, we used the MAE because we are interested in knowing the degree of error in predicting the number of nodes and edges that the model infers from the image. \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \text{MAE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}|y_i - \hat{y}_i| \text{NMAE} = 1 - \min\left(\frac{\text{MAE}}{\text{Range}}, 1\right) \quad, \text{where} \text{Range} = \begin{cases} \max(y) - \min(y) \text{if } \max(y_i) \neq \min(y_i) 1 \text{if } \max(y_i) = \min(y_i) \end{cases} \end{aligned}\end{equation} Hereby, $y_i$ is the actual number of elements (either nodes or edges) that appear in the image, while $\hat{y _i$ is the value predicted by the model.}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation} MAE = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}|y_i - \hat{y}_i| NMAE = 1 - \min\left(\frac{MAE}{Range}, 1\right) \quad, where Range =  \max(y) - \min(y) if  \max(y_i) \neq \min(y_i) 1 if  \max(y_i) = \min(y_i)  \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-246: Moreover, NMAE is the normalization of MAE---using \text{Range ---to ensure that predictions closer to the true values approach a value of 1, while those further away approach 0.}
elements.sent-2411-247: Following \citet{Zou2024-tt , we assess model responses in tasks 2-5, and 7 using an accuracy metric, but we extended it to account for both exact and partial matches.}
elements.sent-2411-248: The accuracy is calculated as: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \text{Accuracy} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \quad, \text{where} m_i = \begin{cases} 1 \text{if } y_i = \hat{y}_i 0.5 \text{if } y_i \text{ partially matches } \hat{y}_i 0 \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \end{aligned}\end{equation} This formulation allows us to account for partial correctness in tasks where a prediction may be partially correct.
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation} Accuracy = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \quad, where m_i =  1 if  y_i = \hat{y}_i 0.5 if  y_i  partially matches  \hat{y}_i 0 otherwise  \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-249: For instance, in task 5 (link prediction), an accuracy score of 0.5 is assigned if only one of the two nodes of the missing edge is correctly predicted by the model.
elements.sent-2411-250: The same principle applies to other tasks where partial matches are possible.
elements.sent-2411-251: Task 6 (reasoning) requires the model to find a shortest path regarding a sequence of nodes.
elements.sent-2411-252: Therefore, we apply the Jaccard Index to provide a similarity value between the actual nodes that form a true path with the path predicted by the model. \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \text{Jaccard Index} = \frac{|P_\text{true} \cap P_\text{pred}|}{|P_\text{true} \cup P_\text{pred}|} \quad, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $P_\text{true}$ represents the set of nodes in the true path and $P_\text{pred}$ represents the set of nodes in the predicted path.
elements.sent-2411-253: That way, even if a model only correctly predicted four nodes of a five-node path, it would have a higher score than a model that only correctly predicted two nodes out of a five-node path.

elements.sent-2411-358: The multimodal capabilities of LLMs have greatly expanded their potential, with LVLMs standing out as a prime example.
elements.sent-2411-359: With just an API and at a low cost, one can now obtain detailed image analyses, whether for object detection or generating comprehensive image captions.
elements.sent-2411-360: However, tasks that involve vision and inference remain inherently difficult and have a long history in computer vision.
elements.sent-2411-361: To properly evaluate emerging LVLMs, they must be tested against visually complex tasks.
elements.sent-2411-362: This is where flexible geometric structures like graphs become crucial.
elements.sent-2411-363: Graphs can introduce significant complexity by simply altering the spatial arrangement of nodes and edges, or modifying their visual representation.
elements.sent-2411-364: Our study introduced \visgraphvar, a benchmark generator designed to challenge current LVLMs and to serve as a testing platform for future models focused on visual graph inference.
elements.sent-2411-365: Below, we present open questions from our study that warrant further exploration:.
elements.sent-2411-366: Which visual style changes truly impair a model's prediction? Through the dataset of graph images produced with \visgraphvar, we provided evidence showing that visual changes affect inference performance, for example, by adding node labels or modifying the layout.
elements.sent-2411-367: However, identifying exactly which changes harm model performance and quantifying their impact on results remains an open question.
elements.sent-2411-368: We trust \visgraphvar\ can inspire other researchers to delve deeper into these questions---not just for visual graph inference---by conducting comprehensive analyses to clearly identify which visual styles distort predictions.

elements.sent-2411-379: This research evaluated Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) through the lens of graphs, chosen for their rich geometric properties and structural complexity.
elements.sent-2411-380: We introduced \visgraphvar, a customizable benchmark generator designed to explore visual variability across both stylistic elements and graph structures.
elements.sent-2411-381: The framework generates datasets spanning seven distinct tasks (detection, classification, segmentation, pattern recognition, link prediction, reasoning, and matching), providing comprehensive coverage of graph interpretation and analysis capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-382: Our analysis of the \visgraphvar-generated dataset reveals two key findings: first, that visual variability has a substantial impact on LVLM performance, and second, that current LVLM architectures require fundamental enhancements to adequately address the complexities of visual graph interpretation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-383: This would enable more detailed evaluations, offering deeper insights into the capabilities and limitations of LVLMs in visual graph analysis tasks.

elements.sent-2411-387: Through iterative refinement of this approach, we developed the subsequent six prompt pairs.
